Tobacco Census 2025

Author

Moses Fleischman

Aims of this report

This report seeks to describe the 2025 CIFTR data in greater detail and clarify the methods by which analyses and visualizations were created. It is meant to be reviewed after viewing the primary material.

1 About the Data

1.1 Survey Design and Collection

This survey is a long running census of all of the stores, restaurants, bars, and other establishments in the city of Cleveland. It aims to collect information on the accessibility to a variety of products, including healthy foods, as well as drugs and other general products.

The information on these establishments is collected annually during the summer by a team of volunteers. The details are collected primarily via groundtruthing.

How was the status of this store determined? (choice=Groundtruthing) n
Checked 2444
Unchecked 9

For more information on the methods of this study, please visit: http://prchn.org/ciftr/.

On account of the survey being long running, it contains a multitude of establishments that have since closed or changed property type. As of the summer of 2025, the data contains 699 closed establishments and 1754 currently open establishments.

Open n
No 699
Yes 1754

This summary of open establishments can be divided by establishment type, as seen below.

PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data) n
1, Supermarket/Grocery Store 131
10, Drug Store or Pharmacy 17
11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts 21
12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood 15
13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related? 12
2, Convenience store (without gas) 128
3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam’s, Walmart, Target) 4
4, Full-Service Restaurant 542
5, Limited-Service Restaurant 324
6, Bars and liquor stores other drinking establishment 212
7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores) 132
8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores 63
9, Tobacco/Smoking Suppliers 38

The above pie chart contains less establishments than the table above, the reason for this is described in the next section.

2 Tobacco Sales

Establishments that sold tobacco products were counted and are displayed below. Tobacco products considered include the following:

  • Cigarettes

  • Menthol cigarettes

  • ENDS (e-cigarettes)

  • Cigarillos/Little Cigars

  • Nicotine Pouches

  • Roll-Your-Own Tobacco

The following establishments are omitted from this list, as described previously:

  • Restaurants (Full-service and Limited-service/Fast-food)

  • Bars and Liquor Stores

  • Closed Stores/Restaurants

  • Tobacco/Smoking Suppliers

In order to do this, we define a new variable has_tobacco, which is a binary variable, in which 1 means the establishment in question has one of the above defined products.

Then, because this report is concerned primarily with easily accessible sources of tobacco, certain establishments are removed. Closed establishments were removed, along with restaurants and bars, as well as tobacco suppliers. Stores that seemed to primarily sell tobacco (based on signage for a smoke or e-cigarette shop, or other exterior signs) were not further investigated, and simply assumed to carry some form of tobacco or another.

Using our data set we can establish a table with the number of each type of establishment that sells tobacco and calculate a proportion that sell tobacco.

has_tobacco n
0 175
1 348
Establishment Type Tobacco not sold Tobacco sold Proportion
1, Supermarket/Grocery Store 53 78 0.595
10, Drug Store or Pharmacy 13 4 0.235
11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts 21
12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood 14 1 0.067
13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related? 7 5 0.417
2, Convenience store (without gas) 27 101 0.789
3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam’s, Walmart, Target) 4
7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores) 12 120 0.909
8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores 24 39 0.619

In the above table we can see that, for example, 78 of the supermarkets or grocery stores in Cleveland sold tobacco, while 53 did not. This reflects a proportion of 0.595 (or 59.5% of) supermarkets/grocery stores selling tobacco in Cleveland.

3 Tobacco 21

Using this data, we are able to determine how many of the stores that do sell tobacco have proper local, state, or federal Tobacco 21 signage.

Here, we used our cleaned data set and established a binary variable t21, with a 1 meaning that local, state, or federal T21 signage was posted. A value of 1 means that somewhere on the premises signage complying with either local, state, or federal tobacco 21 policies were placed. A value of 0 means that this was not the case.

It was considered whether to omit observations with missing data on t21. The decision was made not to do so in order to keep the data set pristine for future analyses in this report. The majority of observations missing this data did not sell tobacco, save for one observation.

Below, we can see the number of establishments selling tobacco that did / did not have proper Tobacco 21 signage, organized by establishment type, as well as a proportion of those establishments that had proper signage.

t21 n
0 267
1 80
Establishment Type No T21 Signage T21 Signage Present Proportion
1, Supermarket/Grocery Store 64 14 0.179
10, Drug Store or Pharmacy 2 2 0.500
12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood 1
13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related? 3 2 0.400
2, Convenience store (without gas) 77 23 0.230
7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores) 92 28 0.233
8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores 28 11 0.282

For example, 14 of the supermarkets or grocery stores (that sold tobacco) in Cleveland had proper signage, while 64 did not. This corresponds to a proportion of 0.179 (or 17.9%) of supermarkets / grocery stores in Cleveland having proper Tobacco 21 signage while selling tobacco.

The list of establishments that had no Tobacco 21 signage despite selling tobacco, as of the survey time can be accessed at: https://tinyurl.com/CIFTR2025Tobacco21List.

4 Ad Coverage

Using the data, we can determine the number of establishments that had more than 25% of their doors and windows covered by advertisements.

We established a new binary variable ad25, for which a value of 1 means that 25% or more of the windows and doors were covered by advertisements, while a value of 0 means that this was not the case.

A large number of establishments were missing data for this variable, with no obviously discernible pattern among them.

Missing Values

Establishment Type n
1, Supermarket/Grocery Store 16
10, Drug Store or Pharmacy 6
11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts 20
12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood 5
13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related? 7
2, Convenience store (without gas) 22
7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores) 10
8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores 15

Here, we summarized the presence of 25% or more coverage of ads, organized by establishment type, among those establishments that sold tobacco. We also calculated a proportion of those establishments that did not have more than 25% ad coverage among total stores of that type.

Establishment Type Ads Covering < 25% Ads Covering > 25% Proportion
1, Supermarket/Grocery Store 74 41 0.357
10, Drug Store or Pharmacy 9 2 0.182
11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts 1
12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood 8 2 0.200
13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related? 4 1 0.200
2, Convenience store (without gas) 24 82 0.774
3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam’s, Walmart, Target) 4
7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores) 34 88 0.721
8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores 39 9 0.188

For example, 41 of the supermarkets or grocery stores in Cleveland had > 25% of the windows/doors covered with advertisements, while 74 did not. This corresponds to a proportion of 0.357 (or 35.7%) of supermarkets / grocery stores in Cleveland having proper >25% of their windows and doors covered with advertisements.

5 Maps

As a small note about the maps that can be viewed at https://tinyurl.com/CIFTR2025Map, the information displayed comes directly from this data set. Given the street addresses of the establishments, a geocoder was used to calculate the latitude and longitude of each location for placement in GIS. Much of the information, including establishment type, whether or not the establishment sold tobacco or had t21 signage, more was calculated exactly as above before being geocoded.

title: "Tobacco Census 2025"

## Aims of this report

This report seeks to describe the 2025 CIFTR data in greater detail and clarify the methods by which analyses and visualizations were created. It is meant to be reviewed after viewing the primary material.
:::

```{r setup}
#| include: FALSE

# Loading important packages

library(gt) # Used for beautifying output tables
library(tidyverse) # Crucial for data cleaning
library(easystats) # Crucial for statistical tests
library(paletteer) # Necessary for our color palette

# Establishing the color palette

palette<- c("#d9d9d9", "#1e3050")

# Setting document defaults

theme_set(theme_minimal())
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

```{r data loading}
#| include: false
# Loading the data

tobacco_raw <- read_csv("TobaccoRetail.csv")

tobacco <- tobacco_raw
```

# About the Data

## Survey Design and Collection

This survey is a long running census of all of the stores, restaurants, bars, and other establishments in the city of Cleveland. It aims to collect information on the accessibility to a variety of products, including healthy foods, as well as drugs and other general products.

The information on these establishments is collected annually during the summer by a team of volunteers. The details are collected primarily via groundtruthing.

```{r note on survey data}
# Many fields in the data show either "Checked" or "Unchecked". Simply, "Checked" means an affirmative, while "Unchecked" means a negative or an incomplete field.

# For example, below, the 2444 observations with a "Checked" value refer to that many observations having been surveyed via groundtruthing. Conversely, the 9 the 2444 observations with a "Unchecked" value refer to that many observations having been surveyed via another method.
```

```{r research method}
tobacco |>
  group_by(`How was the status of this store determined? (choice=Groundtruthing)`) |>
  count() |>
  print_md()
```

For more information on the methods of this study, please visit: *http://prchn.org/ciftr/*.

On account of the survey being long running, it contains a multitude of establishments that have since closed or changed property type. As of the summer of 2025, the data contains 699 closed establishments and 1754 currently open establishments.

```{r open closed}
tobacco |>
  mutate(Open = case_when(`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "99, Closed" ~ "No", TRUE ~ "Yes")) |>
  group_by(Open) |>
  count() |>
  print_md()
```

This summary of open establishments can be divided by establishment type, as seen below.

```{r grouping by type}
#| echo: FALSE

tobacco_count <- tobacco |>
  filter(`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` != "99, Closed") |>
  group_by(`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)`) |>
  count()

tobacco_count |>
  print_md()
```

```{r pie chart}
est_piechart <- ggplot(tobacco_count |>
         filter(!`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` %in% c("4, Full-Service Restaurant", "5, Limited-Service Restaurant", "6, Bars and liquor stores other drinking establishment", "9, Tobacco/Smoking Suppliers")) |>
         mutate(`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` = case_when(`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores" ~ "Discount Stores", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam's, Walmart, Target)" ~ "Warehouses", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores)" ~ "Gas Stations", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts" ~ "Bakeries/Candy", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related?" ~ "Miscellaneous", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "2, Convenience store (without gas)" ~ "Convenience Store", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood" ~ "Butcher/Seafood", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "1, Supermarket/Grocery Store" ~ "Supermarket/Grocery", `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)` == "10, Drug Store or Pharmacy" ~ "Pharmacy", TRUE ~ `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)`)), 
  aes(x = "", y = n, fill = `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)`)) +
  labs(fill = "Establishment Type") +
  geom_bar(width = 1, stat = "identity", color = "white") +
  guides(color = "none") +
  coord_polar("y", start = 0) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Establishments in Cleveland") +
  scale_fill_paletteer_d("palettetown::latios")

est_piechart
```

The above pie chart contains less establishments than the table above, the reason for this is described in the next section.

```{r pie chart save}
#| eval: false
#| include: false
ggsave("est_piechart.png", est_piechart)
```

# Tobacco Sales

Establishments that sold tobacco products were counted and are displayed below. Tobacco products considered include the following:

-   Cigarettes

-   Menthol cigarettes

-   ENDS (e-cigarettes)

-   Cigarillos/Little Cigars

-   Nicotine Pouches

-   Roll-Your-Own Tobacco

The following establishments are omitted from this list, as described previously:

-   Restaurants (Full-service and Limited-service/Fast-food)

-   Bars and Liquor Stores

-   Closed Stores/Restaurants

-   Tobacco/Smoking Suppliers


In order to do this, we define a new variable `has_tobacco`, which is a binary variable, in which 1 means the establishment in question has one of the above defined products.

```{r tobacco recode}
tobacco <- tobacco |>
  mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(
    `Cigarettes (choice=Yes)` == "Checked" ~ 1,
    `Cigarettes (choice=Yes)` == "Unchecked" ~ 0
  )) |>
  mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(
    `Menthol Cigarettes (choice=Yes)` == "Checked" ~ 1,
    TRUE ~ has_tobacco
  )) |>
  mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(
    `LCCs (choice=Yes)` == "Checked" ~ 1,
    TRUE ~ has_tobacco
  )) |>
  mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(
    `ENDS (choice=Yes)` == "Checked" ~ 1,
    TRUE ~ has_tobacco
  )) |>
  mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(
    `Nicotine Pouches (choice=Yes)` == "Checked" ~ 1,
    TRUE ~ has_tobacco
  )) |>
  mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(
    `Roll-Your-Own (Loose Leaf) (choice=Yes)` == "Checked" ~ 1,
    TRUE ~ has_tobacco
  ))
```

Then, because this report is concerned primarily with easily accessible sources of tobacco, certain establishments are removed. Closed establishments were removed, along with restaurants and bars, as well as tobacco suppliers. Stores that seemed to primarily sell tobacco (based on signage for a smoke or e-cigarette shop, or other exterior signs) were not further investigated, and simply assumed to carry some form of tobacco or another.

```{r removing restaurants/bars/smoke/closed}
tobacco_clean <- tobacco |>
  mutate(`Establishment Type` = as.factor(`PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)`)) |>
  filter(!`Establishment Type` %in% c("6, Bars and liquor stores other drinking establishment","5, Limited-Service Restaurant","4, Full-Service Restaurant", "99, Closed",  
"9, Tobacco/Smoking Suppliers", NA))
```

Using our data set we can establish a table with the number of each type of establishment that sells tobacco and calculate a proportion that sell tobacco.

```{r tobacco count by type}
#| echo: false
tobacco_clean |>
  group_by(has_tobacco) |>
  count() |>
  print_md()

tobacco_summary <- tobacco_clean |>
  group_by(`Establishment Type`, has_tobacco) |>
  count()

pivot_wider(tobacco_summary, names_from = has_tobacco, values_from = n) |>
  mutate(proportion = `1`/(`1`+`0`)) |>
  rename(tobacco_sold = `1`, tobacco_not_sold = `0`) |> 
  mutate(`Tobacco not sold` = tobacco_not_sold, `Tobacco sold` = tobacco_sold, `Proportion` = proportion) |>
  select(-tobacco_sold, -tobacco_not_sold, -proportion) |>
  print_md(digits = 3)
```

In the above table we can see that, for example, 78 of the supermarkets or grocery stores in Cleveland sold tobacco, while 53 did not. This reflects a proportion of 0.595 (or 59.5% of) supermarkets/grocery stores selling tobacco in Cleveland.

```{r}
# We use a slightly different method to create essentially the same table as above, but in a way that is more understandable to the computer for our visualization

tobacco_props <- tobacco_clean %>%
  group_by(`Establishment Type`) |>
  count(has_tobacco) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  mutate(sum = sum(n)) |>
  mutate(proportion = round(proportion * 100, digits = 2), proportion = as.character(proportion), proportion = case_when(has_tobacco == 0 ~ "", TRUE ~ paste0(proportion, "%")))

tobacco_sold_bar <- ggplot(tobacco_props |> mutate(has_tobacco = case_when(has_tobacco == 0 ~ "No Tobacco Sold", has_tobacco == 1 ~ "Tobacco Sold")) |>
         mutate(has_tobacco = as.factor(has_tobacco)) |>
         mutate(`Establishment Type` = case_when(`Establishment Type` == "8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores" ~ "Discount Stores", `Establishment Type` == "3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam's, Walmart, Target)" ~ "Warehouses", `Establishment Type` == "7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores)" ~ "Gas Stations", `Establishment Type` == "11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts" ~ "Bakeries/Candy", `Establishment Type` == "13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related?" ~ "Miscellaneous", `Establishment Type` == "2, Convenience store (without gas)" ~ "Convenience Store", `Establishment Type` == "12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood" ~ "Butcher/Seafood", `Establishment Type` == "1, Supermarket/Grocery Store" ~ "Supermarket/Grocery", `Establishment Type` == "10, Drug Store or Pharmacy" ~ "Pharmacy", TRUE ~ `Establishment Type`)),
  aes(x = reorder(`Establishment Type`, -n), fill = has_tobacco, y = n)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip()+
  geom_text(aes(y = sum + 10,label = proportion),
            colour = "black", fontface = "bold", size = 3) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.75, 0.8))+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank())+
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
  xlab("Establishment Type") +
  ylab("Count") +
  labs(fill = "Tobacco Sold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette)

tobacco_sold_bar

ggsave("tobacco_sold_bar.png", tobacco_sold_bar, width = 8, height = 4)
```

# Tobacco 21

Using this data, we are able to determine how many of the stores that do sell tobacco have proper local, state, or federal Tobacco 21 signage.

Here, we used our cleaned data set and established a binary variable `t21`, with a 1 meaning that local, state, or federal T21 signage was posted. A value of 1 means that somewhere on the premises signage complying with either local, state, or federal tobacco 21 policies were placed. A value of 0 means that this was not the case.

It was considered whether to omit observations with missing data on `t21`. The decision was made not to do so in order to keep the data set pristine for future analyses in this report. The majority of observations missing this data did not sell tobacco, save for one observation.

```{r t21 setup}
#| include: false

tobacco_clean <- tobacco_clean |>
  mutate(t21 = case_when(`Is there local, state, or federal T21 signage posted?`== "Yes" ~ 1,
                         `Is there local, state, or federal T21 signage posted?` == "No" ~ 0, 
                         TRUE ~ NA)) |>
  mutate(`Establishment Type` = `PRCHN Structural Classification (as of 2024 data)`)

tobacco_clean |>
  filter(is.na(t21)) |>
  select(`Retail Name`, `Establishment Type`, t21, has_tobacco) |>
  group_by(has_tobacco) |>
  count() |>
  print_md()

tobacco_clean |>
  filter(is.na(t21)) |>
  select(`Retail Name`, t21, has_tobacco) |>
  filter(has_tobacco == 1)
```

Below, we can see the number of establishments selling tobacco that did / did not have proper Tobacco 21 signage, organized by establishment type, as well as a proportion of those establishments that had proper signage.

```{r t21 table}
#| echo: false

t21_summary <- tobacco_clean |>
  filter(has_tobacco == 1) |>
  filter(is.na(t21) == FALSE) |>
  group_by(`Establishment Type`, t21) |>
  count()

tobacco_clean |>
  filter(has_tobacco == 1) |>
  filter(is.na(t21) == FALSE) |>
  group_by(t21) |>
  count() |>
  print_md()

t21_props <- tobacco_clean |>
  filter(has_tobacco == 1) |>
  filter(is.na(t21) == FALSE) |>
  group_by(`Establishment Type`) |>
  count(t21) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  mutate(sum = sum(n)) |>
  mutate(proportion = round(proportion * 100, digits = 2), proportion = as.character(proportion), proportion = case_when(t21 == 0 ~ "", TRUE ~ paste0(proportion, "%")))


t21_summary  |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = t21, values_from = n) |>
  mutate(proportion = `1`/(`1`+`0`)) |>
  rename(T21_signage = `1`, no_T21_signage = `0`) |> 
  mutate(`No T21 Signage` = no_T21_signage, `T21 Signage Present` = T21_signage, `Proportion` = proportion) |>
  select(-no_T21_signage, -T21_signage, -proportion) |>
  print_md(digits = 3)
```

For example, 14 of the supermarkets or grocery stores (that sold tobacco) in Cleveland had proper signage, while 64 did not. This corresponds to a proportion of 0.179 (or 17.9%) of supermarkets / grocery stores in Cleveland having proper Tobacco 21 signage while selling tobacco.

```{r t21 bar}
t21_bar <- ggplot(t21_props |>
                    mutate(t21 = case_when(t21 == 0 ~ "No Signage", t21 == 1 ~ "Signage Posted")) |>
                    mutate(t21 = as.factor(t21)) |>
                    mutate(`Establishment Type` = case_when(`Establishment Type` == "8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores" ~ "Discount Stores", `Establishment Type` == "3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam's, Walmart, Target)" ~ "Warehouses", `Establishment Type` == "7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores)" ~ "Gas Stations", `Establishment Type` == "11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts" ~ "Bakeries/Candy", `Establishment Type` == "13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related?" ~ "Miscellaneous", `Establishment Type` == "2, Convenience store (without gas)" ~ "Convenience Store", `Establishment Type` == "12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood" ~ "Butcher/Seafood", `Establishment Type` == "1, Supermarket/Grocery Store" ~ "Supermarket/Grocery", `Establishment Type` == "10, Drug Store or Pharmacy" ~ "Pharmacy", TRUE ~ `Establishment Type`)),
  aes(x = reorder(`Establishment Type`, -n), fill = t21, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack", stat="identity")+
  coord_flip()+
  geom_text(aes(y = sum + 7,label = proportion),
            colour = "black", fontface = "bold", size = 3) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.75, 0.8))+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank())+
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  xlab("Establishment Type") +
  ylab("Number in Cleveland") +
  labs(fill = "Tobacco 21 Signage") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette)

t21_bar
```

```{r t21 bar save}
ggsave("t21_bar.png", t21_bar, width = 8, height = 4)
```

The list of establishments that had no Tobacco 21 signage despite selling tobacco, as of the survey time can be accessed at: *https://tinyurl.com/CIFTR2025Tobacco21List*.

```{r write t21 list}
#| eval: false
#| include: false

tobacco_clean |>
  filter(has_tobacco == 1) |>
  filter(t21 == 0) |>
  select(`Retail Name`, `Unique Street Address`, has_tobacco, t21) |>
  write_csv("tobacco21.csv")
```

# Ad Coverage

Using the data, we can determine the number of establishments that had more than 25% of their doors and windows covered by advertisements.

We established a new binary variable `ad25`, for which a value of 1 means that 25% or more of the windows and doors were covered by advertisements, while a value of 0 means that this was not the case.

```{r ad25}
#| echo: false

tobacco_clean <- tobacco_clean |>
  mutate(ad25 = case_when(`Is the percentage of windows/doors covered by any ads 25% or more?` == "Yes" ~ 1,
                         `Is the percentage of windows/doors covered by any ads 25% or more?` == "No" ~ 0,
         TRUE ~ NA))
```

A large number of establishments were missing data for this variable, with no obviously discernible pattern among them.

**Missing Values**
```{r ad25 NA}
tobacco_clean |>
  filter(is.na(ad25)) |>
  group_by(`Establishment Type`) |>
  count() |>
  print_md()
```

Here, we summarized the presence of 25% or more coverage of ads, organized by establishment type, among those establishments that sold tobacco. We also calculated a proportion of those establishments that did not have more than 25% ad coverage among total stores of that type.

```{r ad25 table}
#| echo: false

ad25_summary <- tobacco_clean |>
  filter(is.na(ad25) == FALSE) |>
  group_by(`Establishment Type`, ad25) |>
  count()

ad25_summary |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = ad25, values_from = n) |>
  mutate(Proportion = `1`/(`1`+`0`)) |>
  rename(`Ads Covering > 25%` = `1`, `Ads Covering < 25%` = `0`) |> print_md(digits = 3)
```

```{r ad25 bar}
ad25_props <- tobacco_clean |>
  filter(is.na(ad25) == FALSE) |>
  group_by(`Establishment Type`) |>
  count(ad25) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  mutate(sum = sum(n)) |>
  mutate(proportion = round(proportion * 100, digits = 2), proportion = as.character(proportion), proportion = case_when(ad25 == 0 ~ "", TRUE ~ paste0(proportion, "%")))

ad25_bar <- ggplot(ad25_props |>
                    mutate(ad25 = case_when(ad25 == 0 ~ "< 25% Coverage", ad25 == 1 ~ "> 25% Coverage")) |>
                    mutate(ad25 = as.factor(ad25)) |>
                    mutate(`Establishment Type` = case_when(`Establishment Type` == "8, Department Stores / General Merchandise Stores" ~ "Department Stores", `Establishment Type` == "3, Warehouse or Supercenters (Costco, Sam's, Walmart, Target)" ~ "Warehouses", `Establishment Type` == "7, Gas Stations (with or without full convenience stores)" ~ "Gas Stations", `Establishment Type` == "11, Bakeries, Candy (confectionary) or Nuts" ~ "Bakeries/Candy", `Establishment Type` == "13, Misc Non-Food or Tobacco Related?" ~ "Miscellaneous", `Establishment Type` == "2, Convenience store (without gas)" ~ "Convenience Store", `Establishment Type` == "12, Butcher/Meat or Seafood" ~ "Butcher/Seafood", `Establishment Type` == "1, Supermarket/Grocery Store" ~ "Supermarket/Grocery", `Establishment Type` == "10, Drug Store or Pharmacy" ~ "Pharmacy", TRUE ~ `Establishment Type`)),
  aes(x = reorder(`Establishment Type`, -n), fill = ad25, y = n)) +
  geom_bar(position = "stack", stat="identity")+
  coord_flip()+
  theme(legend.position = c(0.75, 0.8))+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank())+
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) +
  geom_text(aes(y = sum + 7,label = proportion),
            colour = "black", fontface = "bold", size = 3) +
  xlab("Establishment Type") +
  ylab("Count") +
  labs(fill = "Advertisement Coverage") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = palette)

ad25_bar
```

```{r ad25 save}
ggsave("ad25_bar.png", ad25_bar, width = 8, height = 4)
```

For example, 41 of the supermarkets or grocery stores in Cleveland had > 25% of the windows/doors covered with advertisements, while 74 did not. This corresponds to a proportion of 0.357 (or 35.7%) of supermarkets / grocery stores in Cleveland having proper >25% of their windows and doors covered with advertisements.

# Maps

As a small note about the maps that can be viewed at *https://tinyurl.com/CIFTR2025Map*, the information displayed comes directly from this data set. Given the street addresses of the establishments, a geocoder was used to calculate the latitude and longitude of each location for placement in GIS. Much of the information, including establishment type, whether or not the establishment sold tobacco or had t21 signage, more was calculated exactly as above before being geocoded.
